Supabase vs Vercel: Which Is Better?

Choosing between Supabase and Vercel? Here's a side-by-side comparison to help you decide.

Quick verdict: Choose Supabase if you need solo-devs or startups. Choose Vercel if you prioritize solo-devs or frontend-developers.

Supabase

PricingFree / $25/mo Pro / $599/mo Team
Rating★★★★½ 4.5/5

Key Features

  • PostgreSQL database with SQL editor and table views
  • Authentication with email, OAuth, magic links, and multi-factor auth
  • Storage for files with S3-compatible API and CDN
  • Real-time subscriptions via WebSocket for live data syncing
  • Edge Functions for serverless compute at the edge
  • Auto-generated TypeScript SDK from your database schema

Pros

  • Free tier is genuinely generous — 2 projects, 500MB database, 50K monthly active users, 5GB bandwidth
  • PostgreSQL is a proper database, not a NoSQL toy; you get real SQL joins, indexes, and advanced queries
  • Auth is built-in and supports email/password, OAuth (Google, GitHub, etc.), and magic links out of the box
  • Real-time subscriptions are surprisingly easy to set up and work well for collaborative features
  • Open-source with a fair license means no vendor lock-in — you can self-host if needed

Cons

  • Scaling costs can be unpredictable; the jump from Free ($0) to Pro ($25) is fine, but Team ($599) is steep
  • Still maturing — some features (like Edge Functions) feel less polished than AWS Lambda or Vercel's offerings
  • Row Level Security (RLS) is powerful but complex; the learning curve for secure app setup is real
  • Database performance at scale requires PostgreSQL tuning knowledge that many developers don't have
  • The dashboard and UI, while improving, occasionally has reliability issues and slow loads

Vercel

PricingFree / $20/mo Pro / custom Enterprise
Rating★★★★½ 4.6/5

Key Features

  • Git-integrated automatic deployments with instant rollbacks
  • Serverless Functions and Edge Functions for backend logic
  • Preview deployments per branch or commit
  • Analytics with real-time user and performance data
  • Automatic SSL certificates and custom domain management
  • ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) for hybrid static/dynamic content

Pros

  • Developer experience is excellent — Git push deploys, instant rollbacks, and automatic SSL just work
  • Free tier is generous enough for personal projects, portfolio sites, and even some production apps
  • Preview deployments for every branch make code review and client approvals much smoother
  • Edge Functions and Edge Config let you move computation closer to users with Next.js integration
  • Analytics and speed insights give real visibility into performance without extra tooling

Cons

  • Usage-based pricing for Pro means costs can surprise you if your app gets popular (bandwidth, function execution)
  • Lock-in concerns — Vercel-optimized features (ISR, Edge Functions) are easier to use on Vercel than elsewhere
  • Serverless cold starts are still a thing; Edge Functions help but aren't a complete solution
  • The platform is optimized heavily for Next.js; other frameworks work but feel like second-class citizens
  • Customer support on the free tier is essentially non-existent; Pro gets you priority but it's not cheap
Try Vercel →

Which Should You Choose?

Both Supabase and Vercel are excellent tools in their category. The right choice depends on your specific needs:

  • Choose Supabase if: Supabase is the open-source Firebase alternative that gives you a PostgreSQL database, authentication, storage, and real-time subscriptions in one package. It's popular with solo developers and startups who want the convenience of a backend-as-a-service without the proprietary lock-in. The generous free tier doesn't hurt either.
  • Choose Vercel if: Vercel is the deployment platform that makes frontend developers feel like they're cheating. Push to GitHub, it deploys automatically, you get a URL. That's it. It handles preview deployments, serverless functions, and edge rendering with minimal configuration. The free tier is absurdly generous, which is why it's everywhere in the indie dev world.
We may earn a commission if you purchase through links. All opinions are our own.
